commit 728532884a74dc41eb5509699cfff8d53e7f1f4f
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb

When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() will keep the hugetlb
folio in pagecache with refcount increased and PG_hwpoison set. This patch
forces a refcount put of the hwpoisoned hugetlb during memory offline to
allow it to be dissolved.

> diff --git a/mm/hugetlb.c b/mm/hugetlb.c
> --- a/mm/hugetlb.c
> +++ b/mm/hugetlb.c

[ ... ]

>  	for (pfn = start_pfn; pfn < end_pfn; pfn += 1 << order) {
>  		folio = pfn_folio(pfn);
> +
> +		/*
> +		 * For hwpoisoned hugetlb, put the refcount increaed by
> +		 * memory-failure, make it succeed to dissolve.
> +		 */

This isn't a bug, but there is a typo (increaed) in the comment above.

> +		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)

Will this successfully detect hwpoisoned tail pages?

According to the MM large folio page flag policy, PG_hwpoison uses the
PF_ANY policy and is set on the specific corrupted subpage.

If a memory failure occurs on a tail page of the hugetlb folio, PG_hwpoison
is set on that tail page and PG_has_hwpoisoned (PF_SECOND) is set on the
head page. In this scenario, folio_test_hwpoison(folio) evaluates to false,
skipping the logic for tail page memory failures.

> +				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
> +			folio_put(folio);

Is it safe to check the reference count locklessly here before calling
folio_put()?

If the last legitimate reference to the folio is dropped concurrently by
another CPU (for example, due to file eviction or unmapping) exactly between
the condition check and the execution of folio_put(), it will execute
on a folio that already has a refcount of 0.

Could this underflow the reference count to -1, corrupting the reference
count state?

Also, what happens if this offline operation fails and is retried from
user space via sysfs?

Since dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios() is called during memory offlining, it
blindly calls folio_put() on hwpoisoned hugetlb folios. If the folio is
still actively in use in the page cache with the hugetlbfs file still open
(so refcount > 1), dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() will fail and abort the
offline attempt.

The reference dropped by folio_put() is never restored. By repeatedly
retrying the memory offline operation, could an administrator artificially
drive the folio's refcount down to 0, resulting in a use-after-free
vulnerability when the folio is returned to the buddy allocator?

> +
>  		rc = dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio);
>  		if (rc)
>  			break;

Target Commit:
commit 728532884a74dc41eb5509699cfff8d53e7f1f4f
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 21 02:10:31 2026 +0000

    mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb
    
    When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() will keep the
    hugetlb folio in pagcahe with refcount increased and PG_hwpoison set. Even
    after the hugetlb file is deleted, the hugetlb folio is still leaked.
    
    If we want to offline the memory block that the hwpoisoned hugetlb folio
    belongs to, it fails in dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios() due to the
    hwpoisoned hugetlb folio isn't free.
    
    I can reproduce this issue with the following steps in qemu:
     1) echo offline >/sys/devices/system/memory/auto_online_blocks
     2) in qemu monitor:
           object_add memory-backend-ram,id=mem10,size=1G
           device_add pc-dimm,id=dimm1,memdev=mem10,node=2
     3) echo online_movable > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/memory136/state
     4) echo 5 > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/hugepages/hugepages-2048kB/nr_hugepages
     5) run ./hugetlb_file. This process will receive SIGBUS.
     6) remove the hugetlbfs file.
     7) echo offline > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/memory136/state
    
    hugetlb_file.c:
      fd = open("/dev/hugepages/my_hugepage_file", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0755);
      fallocate(fd, 0, 0, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2);
      addr = mmap(NULL, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
                    MAP_SHARED | MAP_HUGETLB, fd, 0);
      memset(addr, 0xaa, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2);
      madvise(addr, HUGEPAGE_SIZE, MADV_HWPOISON);
    
    To fix it, force to put ref of hwpoisoned hugetlb in memory offline, the
    hwpoisoned hugetlb will be freed and succeeds to be dissolved. We couldn't
    avoid races here, just like commit b023f46813cd ("memory-hotplug: skip
    HWPoisoned page when offlining pages"), which force to skip hwpoisoned
    page regardless of refcount.
    
    Signed-off-by: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

diff --git a/mm/hugetlb.c b/mm/hugetlb.c
index 88009cd2a846..1fb038d06a83 100644
--- a/mm/hugetlb.c
+++ b/mm/hugetlb.c
@@ -2087,6 +2087,15 @@ int dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios(unsigned long start_pfn, unsigned long end_pfn)
 
 	for (pfn = start_pfn; pfn < end_pfn; pfn += 1 << order) {
 		folio = pfn_folio(pfn);
+
+		/*
+		 * For hwpoisoned hugetlb, put the refcount increaed by
+		 * memory-failure, make it succeed to dissolve.
+		 */
+		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
+				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
+			folio_put(folio);
+
 		rc = dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio);
 		if (rc)
 			break;

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Proposed Hugetlb Folio Memory Offline Fix**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit, and my initial thought is, "This looks like a recipe for disaster." The issue is a memory offline failure triggered by a hardware-poisoned hugetlb folio. I understand that `me_huge_page()` is the culprit, increasing the folio's refcount and setting `PG_hwpoison`. The file might be deleted, but the hugetlb folio lingers because its refcount isn't zero. Then, when the system attempts to offline the memory block, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` is called, but it fails because the folio's refcount is greater than zero and therefore it is not considered free.

The patch proposes modifying `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` to include a conditional `folio_put(folio)` for hwpoisoned hugetlb folios. My immediate reaction? That's risky. The code checks `folio_ref_count(folio)`, and if it's not zero, it calls `folio_put`. But if the refcount is greater than one, `folio_put` just decrements it! What if the refcount is actually 1? Calling `folio_put` here without any prior reference could lead to freeing the folio and a potential use-after-free situation, particularly if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` is then called on a freed folio! Or what if another thread *is* still holding a reference? This code would just blindly drop *their* reference!

The commit message tries to justify this by referencing a previous commit, b023f46813cd, which skips a hwpoisoned page during offlining, like it's the same thing. But hold on a second! Skipping a page is completely different from forcibly dropping a reference using `folio_put(folio)`! If you drop a reference you don't own, you could free the page while someone else is using it. If that other thread is still accessing it, this is a use-after-free scenario. Even worse, if they *later* call `folio_put`, you've created a refcount underflow! The commit message says that `me_huge_page()` keeps the folio in the pagecache with its refcount increased. So dropping the refcount with `folio_put` would mean the pagecache still has a pointer to the now-decremented page. When the pagecache entry is eventually removed, it will call `folio_put` on a page with a negative refcount, causing a crash!

The commit message mentions that even after the file is deleted, the folio "leaks". If the file is deleted, shouldn't the pagecache entry for the folio be removed? Could `me_huge_page()` have taken some additional reference? I need to go look into `mm/memory-failure.c` and figure out how `me_huge_page()` interacts with refcounts. I should search for `me_huge_page`. This patch is concerning and requires a much closer look at how references are managed in the context of hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios.

Okay, so `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` simply returns 0. That means `truncate_error_folio` gets a zero `err` and proceeds to call `filemap_release_folio`. I see that `filemap_release_folio` doesn't actually remove the folio from the page cache; it just attempts to release private buffers. Interesting. Now let's dive back and look at `truncate_error_folio`. The thing is `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` returns 0, and `filemap_release_folio` *probably* returns true (since Hugetlb pages rarely have private buffers), `truncate_error_folio` is returning `MF_RECOVERED`. *But* it doesn't really remove the folio from the page cache!

Now, the `generic_error_remove_folio` calls `truncate_inode_folio`, but `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` does *nothing*. This must mean `me_huge_page` is setting `extra_pins = true`, and keeps the folio in the page cache!

Now, when the memory block is offlined, we hit `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`. It iterates over pages. If the Hugetlb folio is hwpoisoned, it forces a `folio_put(folio)`. But hang on - if it was in the page cache, the page cache *still* has a reference to it! And the XArray still has a pointer! So calling `folio_put(folio)` here is dropping the page cache's reference.

Then, if the folio refcount drops to zero, it is freed, but the page cache still has a pointer. This is a classic Use-After-Free! When the file is later deleted or truncated, the page cache will try to free the folio, calling `folio_put()` again which underflows the refcount or accesses freed memory. The commit message even says, "Even after the hugetlb file is deleted, the hugetlb folio is still leaked."

Why is this? When the file is deleted, `remove_inode_hugepages` is called, which should clear the page cache references. Does `remove_inode_hugepages` drop the extra pin that `me_huge_page` added? Let's check `me_huge_page`. `get_hwpoison_page` successfully took a reference. So the refcount is elevated by +1 because of memory failure handling! The memory failure handling *explicitly* holds a reference to the page if it's hwpoisoned, and *never* drops it! `get_any_page` calls `__get_hwpoison_page`, which calls `folio_try_get`. Then `me_huge_page` will: if a mapping exists (so if it's in the page cache), it calls `truncate_error_folio`, and explicitly *doesn't* call `folio_put(folio)`! It just unlocks the folio, leaving the refcount elevated! So the memory-failure subsystem deliberately holds a reference to the hwpoisoned Hugetlb folio *forever*!

The commit message points to this: "When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() will keep the hugetlb folio in pagcahe with refcount increased and PG_hwpoison set. Even after the hugetlb file is deleted, the hugetlb folio is still leaked." `me_huge_page` left the refcount elevated, and `remove_inode_hugepages` drops the page cache's reference. But the folio refcount is still at least 1 (the memory-failure refcount).

So the folio isn't freed. It stays around, hwpoisoned, in the page cache.

Now, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` is called (during memory offline). It sees this folio with `refcount == 1`. The author wants to "fix" this by simply calling `folio_put(folio)` in `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`. What if the file is *not* yet deleted?

If the file isn't deleted, the page cache *still* holds a reference, making the refcount 2 or more. The patch says: `if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio) && folio_ref_count(folio))) folio_put(folio);`

If the refcount is 2, it drops to 1. If it's 1, it drops to 0. But it doesn't check if it's still in the page cache!

    "thought": "**Analysis of a Critical Refcount Bug in Hugetlb Memory Offlining**

Okay, let me break this down. My immediate reaction is alarm. The core issue revolves around this new `folio_put(folio)` call within `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` and how it interacts with the memory failure and page cache subsystems. The patch attempts to handle hwpoisoned hugetlb pages during memory offlining, which is a tricky area, but it introduces a glaring refcount bug.

First, I see the potential for a double-free or use-after-free scenario. If a folio is hwpoisoned, the memory failure handling presumably adds a reference. The page cache *also* likely holds a reference if the page is mapped to a file. The patch's `folio_put` call in `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` blindly decrements this refcount.  If the file associated with the page is *not* deleted prior to the offline attempt, `folio_put` is dropping the page cache's refcount, which is bad. The subsequent call to `remove_inode_hugepages` is going to find a folio it *thinks* is still valid, but whose refcount has been dropped by this patch, leading to a race condition.

I’m particularly concerned about the loop structure.  This isn't a one-time operation. `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` is called repeatedly during the offline process.  Each call decrements the refcount of *every* hwpoisoned hugetlb page in the target block if its refcount is greater than 0.  This is a blatant refcount drop, which could lead to refcount underflow and free the page prematurely! A user could trigger this simply by repeatedly calling an offline command. This is incredibly dangerous!

The code checks if the page is hwpoisoned and a hugetlb page AND if the refcount is non-zero, it then calls `folio_put`. However, it doesn't consider what owns the reference, so it may drop the refcount on the page cache and free the page when the page cache STILL thinks it has the reference. This leads to a dangling pointer.

The patch author's own test scenario raises even more red flags. Their steps show that depending on the *order* of operations (removing the hugetlb file vs. offlining the block), the memory-failure refcount could become the *only* reference. If the `folio_put` is called at this point, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` will proceed.

Even if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` fails (due to other pages being pinned), the repeated calls can lead to the refcount going down to 0, eventually dissolving the folio! Then, the page cache can still try to access a freed page.

The author mentions a commit, `b023f46813cd`, which skipped hwpoisoned pages to avoid races. I understand why they would choose to skip these pages, as the current patch attempts to do *exactly* the opposite. Instead of skipping the page, it decrements the refcount. This is precisely what's causing the problem!

In summary, this patch is fundamentally flawed. It lacks proper refcount management. It assumes that dropping a reference will lead to the dissolution of the page, even though it may be held by other subsystems (particularly the page cache), and does so without correctly coordinating with the memory-failure subsystem. This blind decrement leads to a critical vulnerability, which can result in use-after-free and potential kernel crashes.

    "content": "{"content":"commit b023f46813cde6e3b8a8c24f432ff9c1fd8e9a64\nAuthor: Wen Congyang <wency@cn.fujitsu.com>\nDate:   Tue Dec 11 16:00:45 2012 -0800\n\n    memory-hotplug: skip HWPoisoned page when offlining pages\n    \n    hwpoisoned may be set when we offline a page by the sysfs interface\n    /sys/devices/system/memory/soft_offline_page or\n    /sys/devices/system/memory/hard_offline_page.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Hugetlb HWPoisoned Page Offlining Path**

Okay, so the core issue here revolves around how `b023f46813cd` handles HWPoisoned pages during memory offlining, specifically when it comes to hugetlb pages.  That commit simply skips HWPoisoned pages without dropping their refcounts.  The problem is, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` expects a zero refcount.  So, we're stuck. We can't just skip it like a regular page because it's a huge page. If we skip, the offline fails because the hugetlb page still spans the memory block and the whole memory block cannot be offlined due to this page.

The commit message acknowledges this race-condition-like situation, but `b023f46813cd` actually *left* the HWPoisoned page behind, marking it as reserved and continuing with the offline. This works for regular pages, but hugetlb pages are different. If it is a hugetlb page, it cannot stay that way! Since a hugetlb page spans multiple pages, they belong to the offlined memory block.  So we *need* to dissolve it into individual pages.

The current patch attempts to dissolve it by artificially dropping the refcount, which allows `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` to proceed.  But now, what happens to that HWPoisoned page after dissolution?

After dissolution, the hugetlb page will become regular order-0 buddy pages. But what happens during freeing?  `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio` calls `free_pages_prepare`.  Ah, right.  `free_pages_prepare` *checks* for `PageHWPoison`!  But... wait, the head page or specific subpage will be HWPoisoned, but we are dissolving it which calls `__free_pages`.  `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` frees all the pages. If one of them has `PG_hwpoison`, the buddy allocator is going to complain!  I can see the `BUG` already.

Okay, let's look at `free_pages_prepare`:

```c
	if (unlikely(PageHWPoison(page)) && !order) {
		/* Do not let hwpoison pages hit pcplists/buddy */
		reset_page_owner(page, order);
		return false;
	}
```

Okay, so if the *order* is 0, we're good. If the order is greater than 0 it will BUG. `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio` frees the pages as *order-0* pages.  So after we call `free_huge_folio` or something similar that either breaks them down or returns them to the buddy, we are okay. This should work because `free_pages_prepare` does the right thing. It is going to reset the owner and skip!

Alright, let's break this down. My main concern boils down to a classic Use-After-Free scenario, triggered by a critical flaw in how we're handling the refcounts of hugetlb folios that have been hit with hardware poisoning.  Specifically, this patch introduces a *blind* decrement of a folio's reference count within a memory offlining routine, without proper synchronization or state tracking.

Here's the gist: `folio_clear_hugetlb_hwpoison` clears the hwpoison flag.  Healthy subpages are broken down and returned to the buddy allocator.  However, when an individual 4K page is marked as `PG_hwpoison`, `free_pages_prepare` intercepts it, preventing it from going back to buddy, which is *exactly* what we want to isolate bad memory.  This is all good when the hugetlb folio is freed, usually when its refcount drops to zero.

Here's where it gets dicey: This *memory-failure* subsystem increases the folio's refcount. The `truncate_error_folio` function is supposed to remove the folio from the page cache.  However, in the case of hugetlbfs, this doesn't actually happen, because `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` *always* returns 0.  Essentially, the page cache *still* holds a reference via `extra_pins = true`. Then the folio stays in the page cache forever, due to `me_huge_page` calls not calling `folio_put` if the folio is mapped.

The ideal scenario is that refcount would drop to 0, which would trigger the hugetlb's destruction, but it never does because of `me_huge_page`.  The page cache holds onto the file! But, that's assuming the file *stays* in the system. When a file is *deleted*, the `remove_inode_hugepages` call removes it and drops its page cache ref.  However, this doesn't account for the `memory_failure` refcount, which is *leaked* by `memory_failure`.

Here's where the patch tries to "help": It introduces `folio_put` inside the memory offline path. And that's our central problem: The memory offlining process is designed to be *retryable*.  If the initial offlining fails (because of other pinned pages or whatever), a user (or, critically, a *script* running with root privileges) might retry the offlining process. Each retry calls `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`, which contains `folio_put`. *Without any check on the state of the refcount.*

Thus, refcount = 1 (memory failure ref), `folio_put` drops it to 0, it's freed!  But the page cache *still* thinks it has it, because `remove_inode_hugepages` hasn't run yet.  A file delete then tries to access it and... Boom! Use-After-Free!  Because memory offlining does not have state tracking for the refs.

The fatal flaw is the *blind* `folio_put(folio)` in the retryable `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` loop. Each time a hwpoisoned hugetlb folio is encountered, its refcount is decremented. If offlining is retried, the refcount gets decremented *multiple* times, leading to underflow or freeing the folio while it is still in the page cache (if the file exists).  Then, when the file is deleted, `remove_inode_hugepages` hits the freed memory: Use-After-Free!

The architecture is also flawed. The problem is a *refcount leak* caused by the `memory_failure` subsystem for hwpoisoned, file-backed huge pages. The patch inappropriately tries to *fix* a leak by blindly dropping a refcount in a completely unrelated subsystem (memory hotplug). The memory offlining code isn't responsible for this leak. The fix should be to remove the reference count when `remove_inode_hugepages` calls `folio_put`. The refcount needs to be dropped once. Not in a retryable loop.

So the real problem is: Blind refcount dropping in a loop without verifying who holds the reference or ensuring it's only dropped *once*.

And, yes, this is a clear UAF.  The vulnerability is exploitable by repeatedly calling memory offline on a poisoned hugetlb folio *before* the associated file is deleted.  A malicious or buggy script running as root could reliably trigger this.

    "thought": "**Vulnerability Analysis of `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` and it's looping through potential hugetlb folios, specifically from `start_pfn` to `end_pfn` in increments of `1 << order`. The code's primary concern here appears to be handling hwpoisoned hugetlb folios during memory offline. The crucial block is this check:

```c
if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
		&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
	folio_put(folio);
```

My initial thought was regarding the safety of `pfn_folio(pfn)`. While valid `pfn`s should work, the increment `1 << order` makes me question what happens if we land on a tail page of a large, non-hugetlb folio. But, `pfn_folio` seems to return the head in this case, meaning `folio_test_hugetlb` can identify the folio correctly.

The core of the issue boils down to the refcount manipulation. The code seems to be handling scenarios where a hugetlb folio is hwpoisoned. The commit message clarifies that `me_huge_page()` retains a reference on hwpoisoned folios, even if the associated file is deleted. So, if the file *is* deleted, the expectation is the page cache drops its refcount, but `me_huge_page()`'s refcount "leaks" a ref, leaving a reference of 1.

The problem arises when the file is *not* deleted yet. If the file is still present, the refcount might be 2 (one from page cache, one from hwpoison). `folio_put` unconditionally decrements the refcount. If the refcount was 2, it becomes 1. Subsequent call to `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` will return -EBUSY. That seems correct according to the code, since the folio is not actually free.

However, the offline process continues and calls `folio_put` on subsequent calls. Now this is concerning. The loop in `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` *doesn't* check if the refcount has already been reduced. It unconditionally decrements it again if `folio_test_hwpoison()`, `folio_test_hugetlb()`, and `folio_ref_count()` are all still true. If the file is not deleted, and memory offline is attempted twice, the second `folio_put()` drops the refcount to zero, while the page cache still has a pointer! This constitutes a clear use-after-free vulnerability!

Let's summarize the scenario:

1.  A hugetlb folio is hwpoisoned and in the page cache. Its refcount is, say, 2 (page cache + `memory_failure`).
2.  Memory offline is attempted (typically by root).
3.  The code checks: `folio_test_hwpoison() && folio_test_hugetlb() && folio_ref_count()` (which is TRUE).
4.  `folio_put(folio)` is executed, decreasing the refcount to 1.
5.  `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` returns `-EBUSY`. The loop breaks. Memory offline fails.
6.  Memory offline is attempted *again*.
7.  The same checks pass again.
8.  `folio_put(folio)` is executed *again*, decreasing the refcount to 0. This is the root cause.
9.  Now, the page cache still has a dangling pointer to a freed folio, leading to a use-after-free.

The fact that `folio_put` is unconditional is a critical design flaw. And because `memory_failure` does a `folio_try_get()` without associating a specific "hwpoison refcount" flag, there is no way to prevent the double `folio_put()`. The `PG_hwpoison` flag remains set, making the second `folio_put()` happen.

The fix relies on *deleting the file first*. If the file is not deleted before the offline, the vulnerability is triggered. Even if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` *succeeds* the hwpoison is still an issue, because the folio would be freed, but `PG_hwpoison` would still be set, which might cause trouble down the line.     "thought": "**Vulnerability Discovered: Arbitrary Hugetlb Folio Refcount Manipulation**

Okay, let's break this down. `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio`...right, it's designed to free a hugetlb folio. The core problem lies in the interaction between this function and `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`, especially when hardware poisoning is involved.

Here's the sequence of thought: The function `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` checks if a hugetlb folio is free. It has a retry loop, and crucially, it *returns -EBUSY* if the refcount isn't zero. The plural version, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`, iterates through a range of pages, and for each folio calls `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio`. *If* a folio is hardware-poisoned *and* the refcount isn't zero, there's a `folio_put(folio)` call *before* calling `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio`.

Here's the critical flaw: If `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` fails (returns a non-zero value, typically because the refcount is still > 0), the loop breaks and the function returns. The `folio_put` call *was* executed though! That's the problem. So, the refcount gets decremented regardless, even if the folio isn't dissolved.

This means if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` fails, for example, because the refcount was 1, and we decremented it to 0. But it was not zero by `memory_failure` and there was another refcount, the refcount is decremented. Next time someone tries to offline memory, the decrement will be called again! This enables an attacker, or a user, to arbitrarily decrement the refcount of a hardware-poisoned hugetlb folio, potentially leading to memory corruption.

The worrying aspect is that there's no lock held *between* the `folio_put` and the call to `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio`. Thus a race condition can occur. Also, if `folio_ref_count(folio)` was 1, it becomes 0. `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` sees it as 0 and successfully dissolves it. But what if the refcount of 1 was NOT from memory_failure?

Then I went down the path of how this interacts with memory failure scenarios. If `PG_hwpoison` is set, `memory_failure` has processed it. If `memory_failure` has isolated it, the page might not be in the page cache anymore.

The commit message about file hugetlb folios having an extra refcount in the page cache with `PG_hwpoison` raised further concerns. If `me_huge_page()` increases the refcount and keeps it in the page cache, then the file is deleted... but then what? Why is it leaked? Wait. Ah, the `truncate` or `evict` operation removes the folio from the page cache, removing its page cache refcount. But is the refcount increased in the page cache? Let's check `me_huge_page`.

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at the `me_huge_page` function and immediately see the potential for a refcount leak. Specifically, if the folio in question is associated with a file hugetlb (indicated by a non-null `mapping`), the code executes `truncate_error_folio()`. That makes sense; we're dealing with a file-backed page. Importantly, `extra_pins` is set to `true`. Then `folio_unlock()` is called.

The key observation is that *`folio_put()` is NOT called* in this `mapping` branch. Now, `memory_failure` has added its own reference to the page via `get_any_page()` (and indirectly `__get_hwpoison_page()`), and I'm realizing this effectively means that the `memory_failure` path holds a single reference on the folio. That reference is never released in the file hugetlb scenario! Bingo, that's where the leak is!

So, the leak is one refcount for file hugetlb folios. Wait... hold on, if we know about the leak, why are we fixing it elsewhere? Why not simply fix it in `memory-failure` directly? By fixing it in `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`, we're only addressing the issue during memory offline. If memory offline *never* occurs, this folio remains leaked indefinitely. It's a fundamental issue.

But, maybe... maybe this is *intentional*? I need to consider this. Is this folio meant to be kept this way? Ah, so the intention here, if `truncate_error_folio()` fails, is to keep the page cached and poisoned, and it looks like this behavior *requires* the leaked refcount. Okay, that context helps a lot. It suggests that there are potentially valid reasons for keeping the page in the cache, and the "leak" is actually a feature, which keeps the page in the pagecache. I should probably dig into `truncate_error_folio()` now to understand the precise failure conditions and the rationale.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Memory Offline Vulnerability in Hugetlb Folios**

Okay, so I've been digging into this code, and it seems like there's a serious flaw in how the kernel handles hwpoisoned hugetlb folios during memory offline operations. My train of thought goes something like this:

First, let's establish the context: if `truncate_error_folio` is called, a page has an extra reference count after error handling. Now, looking at `me_huge_page`, any mapping gets `extra_pins = true`. When memory failure occurs, there's at least a reference count of 2 for hugetlb folios. It's supposed to be left in the page cache because the code *wants* it to stick around. My immediate question is why the code doesn't drop the `get_any_page` refcount. I suspect it's because hwpoisoning. The page is marked poisoned, and that elevated refcount is meant to keep it from being freed before it's dealt with.

The trouble begins when the file associated with a hugetlb folio is deleted. The page cache's reference count drops, and we're left with that `memory_failure` refcount. The folio *isn't* freed back to the hugetlb pool because the refcount is greater than zero. The patch is intended to address this, to permit memory offlining in such cases.

However, the more I look at `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`, the more concerned I get. If an attacker can repeatedly trigger a memory offline, the code *unconditionally* calls `folio_put()` on hwpoisoned hugetlb folios with a non-zero refcount. The first time, it drops the refcount by 1. The code then fails because the refcount wasn't zero. The attacker simply calls offline *again*.

Each time, the code calls `folio_put()`. The critical flaw is that there is *no* check to ensure that the refcount is one and *only* due to memory_failure. It blindly decrements the reference count. If the folio is in the page cache (e.g., the file has *not* been deleted yet), the second offline attempt will drop it to 0, which "frees" the folio while it is still referenced in the page cache! Use-after-free!

If this repeated offline sequence is possible, the attacker can decrement the refcount of *any* hwpoisoned hugetlb folio, potentially resulting in a UAF.

This gets worse with anonymous hugetlb folios. When dealing with an anonymous hugetlb folio (`mapping == NULL`), `me_huge_page` does call `folio_put`. So that "get_any_page" refcount is dropped. But if the folio is mapped by a process (PTE), that mapping has a refcount. Repeatedly offlining can drop that refcount, allowing it to be freed while still mapped. Use-after-free!

It seems like this is a fundamental design flaw. The `folio_put` is acting like a crude workaround to clear a potentially "leaked" reference count *without* properly accounting for where that reference count is coming from. The vulnerability is the user can repeatedly call memory offline. The failure does not prevent further attempts.

I am also worried about tail pages of a hugetlb folio. But the code does, however, use the head page for testing `PG_hwpoison`. It appears this can be avoided. In general, `PF_ANY` means flags are on a per-page basis.

In summary, the issue is that `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` calls `folio_put(folio)` unconditionally on any hwpoisoned hugetlb folio, without regard for where the refcount comes from. This is not prevented by failures. It can cause Use-After-Free situations when used with anonymous hugetlb folios and file-backed hugetlb folios. This needs to be addressed immediately.

We can use the non-atomic version of the test and set\n * operations as both should be shielded with the zone lock to prevent\n * any possible races on the setting or clearing of the bit.\n */\n__PAGEFLAG(Reported, reported, PF_NO_COMPOUND)\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG\nPAGEFLAG(VmemmapSelfHosted, vmemmap_self_hosted, PF_ANY)\n#else\nPAGEFLAG_FALSE(VmemmapSelfHosted, vmemmap_self_hosted)\n#endif\n\n/*\n * On an anonymous folio mapped into a user virtual memory area,\n * folio->mapping points to its anon_vma, not to a struct address_space;\n * with the FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON bit set to distinguish it.  See rmap.h.\n *\n * On an anonymous folio in a VM_MERGEABLE area, if CONFIG_KSM is enabled,\n * the FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON_KSM bit may be set along with the FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON\n * bit; and then folio->mapping points, not to an anon_vma, but to a private\n * structure which KSM associates with that merged folio.  See ksm.h.\n *\n * Please note that, confusingly, \"folio_mapping\" refers to the inode\n * address_space which maps the folio from disk; whereas \"folio_mapped\"\n * refers to user virtual address space into which the folio is mapped.\n *\n * For slab pages, since slab reuses the bits in struct page to store its\n * internal states, the folio->mapping does not exist as such, nor do\n * these flags below.  So in order to avoid testing non-existent bits,\n * please make sure that folio_test_slab(folio) actually evaluates to\n * false before calling the following functions (e.g., folio_test_anon).\n * See mm/slab.h.\n */\n#define FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON\t0x1\n#define FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON_KSM\t0x2\n#define FOLIO_MAPPING_KSM\t(FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON | FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON_KSM)\n#define FOLIO_MAPPING_FLAGS\t(FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON | FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON_KSM)\n\nstatic __always_inline bool folio_test_anon(const struct folio *folio)\n{\n\treturn ((unsigned long)folio->mapping & FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON) != 0;\n}\n\nstatic __always_inline bool folio_test_lazyfree(const struct folio *folio)\n{\n\treturn folio_test_anon(folio) && !folio_test_swapbacked(folio);\n}\n\nstatic __always_inline bool PageAnonNotKsm(const struct page *page)\n{\n\tunsigned long flags = (unsigned long)page_folio(page)->mapping;\n\n\treturn (flags & FOLIO_MAPPING_FLAGS) == FOLIO_MAPPING_ANON;\n}\n\nstatic __always_inline bool PageAnon(const struct page *page)\n{\n\treturn folio_test_anon(page_folio(page));\n}\n#ifdef CONFIG_KSM\n/*\n * A KSM page is one of those write-protected \"shared pages\" or \"merged pages\"\n * which KSM maps into multiple mms, wherever identical anonymous page content\n * is found in VM_MERGEABLE vmas.  It's a PageAnon page, pointing not to any\n * anon_vma, but to that page's node of the stable tree.\n */\nstatic __always_inline bool folio_test_ksm(const struct folio *folio)\n{\n\treturn ((unsigned long)folio->mapping & FOLIO_MAPPING_FLAGS) ==\n\t\t\t\tFOLIO_MAPPING_KSM;\n}\n#else\nFOLIO_TEST_FLAG_FALSE(ksm)\n#endif\n\nu64 stable_page_flags(const struct page *page);\n\n/**\n * folio_xor_flags_has_waiters - Change some folio flags.\n * @folio: The folio.\n * @mask: Bits set in this word will be changed.\n *\n * This must only be used for flags which are changed with the folio\n * lock held.  For example, it is unsafe to use for PG_dirty as that\n * can be set without the folio lock held.  It can also only be used\n * on flags which are in the range 0-6 as some of the implementations\n * only affect those bits.\n *\n * Return: Whether there are tasks waiting on the folio.\n */\nstatic inline bool folio_xor_flags_has_waiters(struct folio *folio,\n\t\tunsigned long mask)\n{\n\treturn xor_unlock_is_negative_byte(mask, folio_flags(folio, 0));\n}\n\n/**\n * folio_test_uptodate - Is this folio up to date?\n * @folio: The folio.\n *\n * The uptodate flag is set on a folio when every byte in the folio is\n * at least as new as the corresponding bytes on storage.  Anonymous\n * and CoW folios are always uptodate.  If the folio is not uptodate,\n * some of the bytes in it may be; see the is_partially_uptodate()\n * address_space operation.\n */\nstatic inline bool folio_test_uptodate(const struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tbool ret = test_bit(PG_uptodate, const_folio_flags(folio, 0));\n\t/*\n\t * Must ensure that the data we read out of the folio is loaded\n\t * _after_ we've loaded folio->flags to check free and in the buddy system\n * (see mm/page_alloc.c).\n */\nPAGE_TYPE_OPS(Buddy, buddy, buddy)\n\n/*\n * PageOffline() indicates that the page is logically offline although the\n * containing section is online. (e.g. inflated in a balloon driver or\n * not onlined when onlining the section).\n * The content of these pages is effectively stale. Such pages should not\n * be touched (read/write/dump/save) except by their owner.\n *\n * When a memory block gets onlined, all pages are initialized with a\n * refcount of 1 and PageOffline(). generic_online_page() will\n * take care of clearing PageOffline().\n *\n * If a driver wants to allow to offline unmovable PageOffline() pages without\n * putting them back to the buddy, it can do so via the memory notifier by\n * decrementing the reference count in MEM_GOING_OFFLINE and incrementing the\n * reference count in MEM_CANCEL_OFFLINE. When offlining, the PageOffline()\n * pages (now with a reference count of zero) are treated like free (unmanaged)\n * pages, allowing the containing memory block to get offlined.     "thought": "**Analysis of a Kernel Memory Offline Patch: A Deep Dive**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial assessment is that this patch introduces some critical flaws related to how we handle hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios during memory offline operations. I'm seeing two significant issues, and they both need immediate attention.

Firstly, there's a serious potential for a Use-After-Free (UAF) condition. The patch unconditionally decrements the reference count of these poisoned hugetlb folios with `folio_put(folio)` during the memory offline process. The crucial problem is that the offline process might fail *after* this decrement (for example if a process still has a reference, resulting in a non-zero refcount), but the decrement itself is *not* reverted. This means we're left with a refcount that doesn't accurately reflect the folio's actual usage.

If the memory offline is retried, the `folio_put` is run again, dropping the refcount arbitrarily low until it finally hits zero. When the refcount *does* reach zero, the folio is dissolved and freed back to the buddy allocator via `free_huge_folio`. The problem is that while this freeing occurs, it is still possible for a process to be mapped to this folio, or the page cache still to hold a reference to this folio (e.g. if the folio is file-backed via hugepages). When that process or the page cache accesses that memory, it will get a page from the buddy allocator, or be corrupted (a clear UAF).

The second critical issue is that the patch's logic is fundamentally flawed because it relies on an incorrect check for hardware-poisoned pages within the hugetlb folio. The `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` function is used. This function, because of how `PF_ANY` is defined for `PG_hwpoison` (via `FOLIO_PF_ANY`), only checks the hardware poisoning *status* of the *head page* of the folio. But, when a tail page in a hugetlb folio is hardware-poisoned, `memory_failure` correctly *sets* the `PG_hwpoison` flag *only* on the poisoned tail page, and sets the `PG_has_hwpoisoned` flag on the head page. Thus, if a tail page is poisoned, `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` returns `false`, causing the patch's intended fix to be skipped entirely, thus being ineffective for *tail* pages. This is a severe bug, and the patch author will need to be made aware of this.

Essentially, the patch is only addressing a very specific, limited case where the *head* page of the huge folio is poisoned. If any other page in the folio is poisoned, the patch does nothing at all. The fix should check if the whole folio is poisoned or if *any* page in the folio is poisoned.

I believe this is a second-order bug as it is caused by the wrong logic.

The commit message and patch author seem to have mistaken "skip" for "put ref", but, in this case, "skip" leaks the page and lets offline proceed, and "put ref" means we *should* fix it, but are actually causing a UAF.

Finally, while investigating this, I also checked for potential race conditions. It doesn't look like any locks protect the folio operation.

In summary, this patch has major problems: an unconditional refcount decrement that can lead to a Use-After-Free vulnerability, and an incorrect check for hwpoisoned pages within hugetlb folios that renders the fix ineffective for the majority of poisoned pages. This needs immediate remediation.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Memory Failure Process in Relation to Huge Pages**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at how `me_huge_page` interacts with the memory failure process, specifically around refcounting and page handling. My initial thought was that `me_huge_page` might be doing something explicit with an extra refcount, or at least not releasing the folio, but that doesn't seem to be the case directly. However, I see it sets `extra_pins = true` if `folio_mapping(folio)` is not NULL. That's the key. So, the question is, what happens with that `extra_pin`?

Ah, okay, `has_extra_refcount` is the critical function. And it *fails* if this `extra_pins` flag is set *and* the refcount isn't what it expects. Which means `extra_pins` *allows* for one extra pin, presumably because the folio is being kept in the page cache. Makes sense.

Hang on... the memory failure *itself* holds a reference to the page! This is crucial. When `memory_failure` is triggered, it calls `get_hwpoison_page`, which I know takes a reference. So that's the connection point.

Now, if `me_huge_page` *succeeds*, does it then drop that reference acquired by the failure process? That's what I need to clarify. I need to dig into the `memory_failure` logic in the context of hugetlb pages to see how it interacts with the refcounts and page state. I'm going to look specifically at the code around `me_huge_page` and `memory_failure` to map out the refcount ownership and the crucial flow here.

    "thought": "**Analysis of hugetlb Folio Reference Counting and Memory Offlining**

Okay, so I'm diving into this `me_huge_page` function and the memory failure process, and I'm seeing a potential issue with hugetlb folio reference counting during memory offlining. First off, I notice that `get_hwpoison_page` takes a reference, which makes sense. Then `me_huge_page` handles the error. If it succeeds, there is a possibility that it will set `ret = MF_RECOVERED` or `MF_FAILED` or `MF_DELAYED` etc. but does it drop the reference after handling?

I'm thinking that `memory_failure` in `mm/memory-failure.c` might not drop the reference if the page is successfully handled, and because the page has `PG_hwpoison` set, the reference count is incremented to keep it safe. Especially for hugetlb pages, `get_hwpoison_page` definitely takes a reference.

Now, if the page is *in use* (mapped by a process, for instance), the reference count should be greater than 1. This is where things get interesting. The patch tries to handle this during memory offlining:

```c
		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
			folio_put(folio);
```

My concern here is that this `folio_put` is unconditional! It doesn't check if `folio_ref_count(folio) == 1`. If `folio_ref_count` is 2, this drops the count to 1, and `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio)` will fail, which is good. But it's not good, it will only fail because `folio_ref_count` is 1. If `folio_ref_count` is 1, and we do `folio_put(folio)`, the count becomes 0, and `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` might free the page.

So if `folio_ref_count` was 2, we just dropped a reference *belonging to someone else* – a use-after-free or reference leak. Is it safe to blindly `folio_put(folio)` just because `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true?

The commit message points out that `me_huge_page` keeps the hugetlb folio in pagecache with an increased refcount and `PG_hwpoison` set. Even after the hugetlb file is deleted, the folio is still leaked because `memory_failure` has taken a refcount. And for file hugetlb it is kept in page cache. If the file is deleted, the page cache ref is dropped, but the hwpoison ref is still there, so refcount is 1.

The patch tries to fix it by putting the refcount during memory offline with that problematic check. But if the file *wasn't* deleted, and it's still mapped or in the page cache, the refcount could be 2. So if we `folio_put(folio)`, we drop the count to 1. `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` returns `-EBUSY` and will still fail. But the refcount is now 1 instead of 2. When the file is deleted, the page cache will do `folio_put`, refcount becomes 0, and the page is freed. But wait, if the page was mapped, `folio_put` drops the refcount.

Here's the problem: what if we offline memory, and there's a hwpoisoned hugetlb folio still in use? `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` is called during memory offline. Let's look at that function:

```c
	for (pfn = start_pfn; pfn < end_pfn; pfn += 1 << order) {
		folio = pfn_folio(pfn);

		/*
		 * For hwpoisoned hugetlb, put the refcount increaed by
		 * memory-failure, make it succeed to dissolve.
		 */
		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
			folio_put(folio);

		rc = dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio);
		if (rc)
			break;
	}
```

    "thought": "**Analysis of Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerability in hugetlb Folio Handling**

Okay, so I've been diving into this `offline_pages` function and the `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` call within it, and it looks like there's a serious refcount issue with hwpoisoned hugetlb folios.  Essentially, the patch I'm examining appears to unconditionally *decrement* the refcount on a hugetlb folio under specific conditions related to hardware poisoning, without proper consideration for all the ways a folio might still be in use.

My thought process led me through the logic:  `offline_pages` has a retry loop that involves `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`.  Within that function, there's a check for hwpoisoned hugetlb folios.  Now, the patch in question contains this:

```c
		/*
		 * For hwpoisoned hugetlb, put the refcount increaed by
		 * memory-failure, make it succeed to dissolve.
		 */
		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
			folio_put(folio);
```

The core issue seems to stem from a premature `folio_put(folio)` call in this block.  If a folio is hwpoisoned, and is a hugetlb page, AND its refcount is greater than zero, it's decremented.  The *problem* is that the refcount can be greater than zero for several reasons, beyond just the hwpoison event itself.  For example, it could be still mapped in a user process's page tables or still be in the page cache.  If this folio is *still* in use, the patch potentially drops the reference too early.

Here's how this could lead to a UAF:  The code might encounter a hwpoisoned hugetlb folio with a refcount of, say, 2 (1 from hwpoison, 1 from the page cache). The patch, seeing that the folio is hwpoisoned and has a non-zero refcount, decrements the refcount.  `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` then checks for refcount zero; it sees refcount *1* and returns `-EBUSY`, preventing the offlining operation. The loop continues.

The problem, as I see it, is that a *retry* of the offlining process will *further* decrement this refcount.  If the user repeatedly retries the operation, this refcount can be decremented to *zero*. Then, when `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` is finally called, the folio is freed while it's still mapped or in the page cache. Subsequent access to this freed memory will then result in a classic use-after-free vulnerability!

Let's go over how the attack sequence will be carried out: create hugetlbfs file, mmap it, MADV_HWPOISON, it will create 3 refcount entries: 1 for page cache, 1 from hwpoison and 1 from mmap. When the first `offline` call fails, refcount would be 2. In subsequent retries, the folio will be freed to buddy allocator. If the kernel tries to read that address in any way it will corrupt the pagecache or cause UAF.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Reference Leaks in HWPoisoned Huge Page Handling**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `me_huge_page` code and trying to understand the implications for hwpoisoned huge pages, especially regarding reference counting. The first part is fairly straightforward: if there's a mapping, `truncate_error_folio` is called. It looks like `truncate_error_folio` *might* unmap the page from the processes, which is key. The comment in the code says the page is kept in the page cache, which means at least two references: one from the page cache and one from the original `get_hwpoison_page`.

So, the critical question is what happens when the huge page file is *not* deleted before offlining the memory block.  If the file *is* deleted, the page cache reference will be dropped, and then the offlining operation can safely decrement the count and free the page. That seems fine.

But if the file *isn't* deleted, then the `folio_ref_count` is 2, one from the page cache, one from `get_hwpoison_page`. The patch has this conditional statement:

```c
		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
			folio_put(folio);
```

This *decrements* the refcount to 1! If this happens, it is a reference leak in reverse! A double free might result.
Now, the `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` call is going to fail with -EBUSY. That's good, right? The offline operation fails. That makes sense but the extra `folio_put` on a hwpoisoned page is where it breaks. This is a reference *leak*. Eventually, when the file is deleted, *another* `folio_put` call from the page cache will be made. The refcount goes to zero. The page will be freed.

This is a problem!  Because of the early `folio_put`, the hugetlb folio is freed while still flagged `PageHWPoison`. But wait, hwpoisoned pages aren't *supposed* to be freed to the buddy allocator. If it goes to the buddy allocator, then `check_new_page` will prevent it from being allocated.

But, `free_pages_prepare` does *not* clear `PageHWPoison`. The check only happens *later* in `check_new_page`, which says that a "bad page" is there! I need to investigate this more.

So, if `folio_ref_count` drops to 0, `__folio_put` is called. For hugetlb pages, `free_huge_folio` is called. Okay, `free_huge_folio` puts it back to the hugetlb pool or, if it's surplus, to the buddy allocator. But if it goes back to the hugetlb pool, it *could* be reallocated! If it is reallocated, this is a serious issue. `free_huge_folio` *should* be checking `PageHWPoison`! Does it?

I need to trace the call paths from `free_huge_folio` and verify that the `PageHWPoison` bit is checked and handled appropriately to prevent potential corruption or re-allocation of poisoned memory. This extra `folio_put` looks dangerous.

It drops a reference strictly because the folio is hwpoisoned, without verifying if the reference actually belongs solely to `memory_failure`. If the hugetlb file is not deleted (unlike the commit's reproducer), the folio remains in the page cache and has a refcount of at least 2. Calling `folio_put()` permanently decrements and leaks the refcount. If the memory offline is triggered repeatedly by a user, the refcount will be artificially driven down to 0. At 0, `free_huge_folio()` frees the folio to the buddy allocator. Since the folio is still in the page cache (or mapped by a process), this causes a severe Use-After-Free and page cache corruption. Additionally, `free_pages_prepare()` will catch the non-NULL `folio->mapping` and trigger a `bad_page` BUG."

Since another CPU could have concurrently reallocated this physical memory as a new hugetlb page, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` might proceed to acquire the `hugetlb_lock` and dissolve a newly allocated folio that belongs to another process, constituting a Time-Of-Check to Time-Of-Use (TOCTOU) race and Use-After-Free."

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Hugetlb Folio Handling During Memory Offlining**

As a seasoned kernel developer, I've dug into this patch and, frankly, I'm seeing a critical vulnerability. The patch introduces an alarming use-after-free scenario and reference corruption within the hugetlb folio handling during memory offlining. Here's the breakdown of my concerns:

**Concern 1: Unconditional Reference Drop causing Use-After-Free**

The core of the problem lies in the unconditional call to `folio_put(folio)` within `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios`. This occurs when the patch detects a hardware-poisoned (hwpoisoned) hugetlb folio and a refcount greater than zero ( `if (... && folio_ref_count(folio))`).  The problem is, this condition is too simplistic.  It blindly drops a reference count without verifying that it belongs solely to the `memory_failure` mechanism. If a hugetlb file is *not* deleted (as is typical), the folio will still be in the page cache, and `folio_ref_count` is at least 2 (one from page cache, one from `memory_failure`). By decrementing the refcount, the code permanently drops the reference count. If the memory offline process is repeatedly triggered, the folio will be prematurely freed via `free_huge_folio()` before its primary users release the resources. Because the folio is still in the page cache (or mapped), this will cause a severe use-after-free and corrupt the page cache's state, resulting in memory corruption. The situation is worsened because even if the page cache doesn't have a reference, `free_pages_prepare()` will detect the active mapping and trigger a `bad_page` BUG check when the folio is freed.

**Concern 2: Use-After-Free of Folio Metadata**

Furthermore, there's a use-after-free issue directly linked to the first. If `folio_put(folio)` *does* drop the *last* reference to the folio, the function `free_huge_folio(folio)` is executed, and the page is returned to the buddy allocator.  Immediately after this, the very next line calls `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio)`. Inside this function,  `folio_test_hugetlb(folio)` is called. At this point, the folio has been freed. Because the physical memory can be reallocated to a new purpose by another CPU at any time, accessing any `folio` state here constitutes a use-after-free vulnerability, which opens a race condition.

In essence, the patch introduces a significant risk. The justification about bypassing hwpoisoned pages in an effort to offline memory does not mitigate the risk caused by a forceful decrement. The author assumed that the folio refcount of `memory_failure` is always equal to one. However, this is not true because the refcount can also belong to the page cache, and can be greater than one. The correct approach would be to safely address all active references and/or check if the refcount is truly *only* the failure's reference.

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch modifying `mm/hugetlb.c`, specifically `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`. The core of the change seems to be this new conditional `folio_put()` call within the loop. The code says: "For hwpoisoned hugetlb, put the refcount increaed by memory-failure, make it succeed to dissolve."

My initial reaction is a red flag. Blindly dropping a reference based solely on `folio_test_hwpoison()`, `folio_test_hugetlb()`, and a non-zero `folio_ref_count()` seems incredibly risky. What if that reference isn't held by the memory failure path? The logic assumes the `folio_ref_count` is solely due to memory failure, but that's a dangerous assumption. `folio_put()` will decrement the count, and if another part of the system is holding a reference, we're looking at a classic Use-After-Free scenario, or at the very least, a corrupted refcount, potentially leading to all sorts of subtle memory corruption down the line.

The commit message touches on the scenario where `me_huge_page()` keeps a hugetlb folio in the pagecache with an increased refcount and `PG_hwpoison` set, even after the hugetlb file is deleted. So, even if the file is gone, the page is "leaked".

Now, let's look at the implementation. The code has:

```c
+		if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio)
+				&& folio_ref_count(folio)))
+			folio_put(folio);
```

Okay, so it checks for `hwpoison`, `hugetlb`, and that the refcount is greater than 0, then calls `folio_put()`. The crucial question is, who *owns* the reference? If the hugetlb file is deleted and the page is in the pagecache, shouldn't the pagecache truncation logic handle dropping references, or maybe the problem lies within `me_huge_page()` itself and how it handles refcounts upon file deletion?

The loop iterates over pfns. So we could have a `hwpoisoned` folio but also another process holding it in the pagetable? And blindly dropping it would be a refcount violation. Even if `memory_failure` tried to unmap it, it might have failed.

Basically, I think blindly dropping the refcount is a very bad idea. It's a fundamental violation of reference counting principles. It's like cutting a thread without knowing who's holding the other end! Even if there's a leak with `me_huge_page()`, the fix shouldn't be here, but in the `me_huge_page()` code, or, the hugetlb file deletion logic, so the page is not leaked.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Use-After-Free in Hugetlbfolio Poison Handling**

Okay, let's break this down. In `me_huge_page()`, if a page has a mapping, we call `truncate_error_folio()` which can return failure, but doesn't release the page cache reference. We then set `extra_pins = true` and unlock the folio. That's a bit odd.

I'm thinking, when does the reference count originate? It's likely from `get_hwpoison_page(p)` when handling memory failures, which gives a +1 reference count for hwpoison isolation. If the page is in the page cache, the cache itself likely has a reference as well (via `folio_nr_pages()` maybe?). Where does `get_hwpoison_page` get released?

Aha! `me_huge_page()` doesn't seem to drop the `get_hwpoison_page` reference if the page is kept in the page cache. And `__page_handle_poison(p)` does `page_ref_inc(p)` for the poison reference, but if the page has a mapping, doesn't drop the *initial* `get_hwpoison_page()` reference. But `memory_failure()` keeps the reference to prevent reallocation while poisoned!

Now, the patch... it adds a check: if it's hwpoisoned, a hugetlb page and has a non-zero refcount, then it calls `folio_put(folio)`. But what if the `folio_ref_count(folio)` is 2? This is the core issue! If we call `folio_put(folio)`, it decrements to 1. Then `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` is called.

If the refcount is > 0, we *just* call `folio_put()`. This is incredibly dangerous. If the folio is in use (e.g., mapped by a task, refcount = 3), we're *stealing* a reference! This will break the refcounting of either the page cache or the task, leading to a Use-After-Free!

The patch author says it's "safe" like another commit that skips hwpoisoned pages, regardless of the refcount. But I don't see how that is safe. They admit we can't avoid races here. They're trying to force the folio to be freed.

If we want to offline memory containing the folio, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` could fail because the hwpoisoned folio isn't free. But, wait, memory offline *calls* `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`. So, if the folio *is* in use (e.g., refcount = 2), we call `folio_put()`. Now `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` might STILL fail because the refcount is 1 (not free). This would happen *because* we stole a reference from someone! This is a severe Use-After-Free bug!

And, wait, it's called exactly once per folio within a loop. Now I need to trace `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` to confirm this.

    "thought": "**My Analysis of a Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerability in HugeTLB Folio Handling**

Okay, here's what I'm seeing in this code. In `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`, we're getting a `folio` using `pfn_folio(pfn)`. The immediate question is, is this `folio` guaranteed to be valid and unfree? I think so, the `pfn_folio()` function should return a real folio. But here's the kicker: we *don't* seem to hold a reference on that `folio`! We're just calling `folio_test_hwpoison()` and other checks.

The problem arises when `folio_put(folio)` is called. That decrements the reference count. If the `folio`'s `refcount` drops to zero, `folio_put` frees the folio. And that's where things get dicey.

The core of the issue is this: memory hotplug offlines memory by iterating through memory blocks. Crucially, it doesn't "own" a reference to the `folio`. If `folio_test_hwpoison()` returns true, this patch *assumes* the `folio_ref_count()` is exactly 1, held by the `memory_failure` logic. But the code *doesn't verify* that assumption. It checks if `folio_ref_count(folio)` is non-zero and *blindly* calls `folio_put(folio)`.

If the `folio_ref_count(folio)` is actually 2, because it is concurrently mapped or used, calling `folio_put()` drops it to 1. This loses a reference, and that could lead to a Use-After-Free somewhere else! Even worse, if the count is 1, but held by someone else, we are still dropping it.

Another problem: what if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` fails? The loop might break, causing memory offline to fail, *but* we've already done `folio_put(folio)`. If another offline attempt runs, it will again call `folio_put(folio)`! Double-free territory!

Let's think about retrying offline. If a page fails, another attempt is made. The next time it sees `folio_test_hwpoison()`, and we've already done `folio_put()`, the refcount is lower. If it went to zero, the folio might have been freed back to the buddy allocator or hugetlb. If buddy got the folio, then it's no longer a hugetlb page, so `folio_test_hugetlb()` would be false. But we know `folio_put()` drops the reference from `memory_failure`, and the hugetlb folio is freed.

Now `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` is called. It checks `!folio_ref_count(folio)`. Then it takes `hugetlb_lock`, removes the page from the hugetlb free lists, and frees it to the buddy allocator.

But wait! Is the folio even on the hugetlb free list at that point? If the refcount was 1, and we do `folio_put()`, `free_huge_folio()` is called! Let's go look at what `free_huge_folio()` actually does. It feels like we're setting up for a really nasty bug here.

    "thought": "**Unconditional Reference Drop Bug in Hugetlb Page Offlining**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and I've got a major problem forming in my mind. `free_huge_folio` either puts the folio back on a freelist or completely drops it.  Fine, makes sense. But then I'm looking at `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` and something's really bugging me. They're looping through physical page frames (`pfn`), calling `pfn_folio(pfn)` in each iteration.

Now, if `folio_ref_count(folio)` is `N`, there's a `folio_put(folio)` being called if the folio is hardware poisoned *and* a hugetlb page. We're dropping *one* reference.  But hold on... if that `N` is 2 because someone else has a reference, we just stole their reference, which leads to a Use-After-Free.

Okay, let's say `N == 1`. We drop the reference, it hits zero, `free_huge_folio()` deals with it, then `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio(folio)` gets called. But, what if the folio wasn't dissolved, maybe because something went wrong, and then we run offline again?  The `folio_ref_count` would now be zero because the initial call of `folio_put()` resulted in it being placed on a freelist. The `if` condition checks `&& folio_ref_count(folio)` and since it is zero, we *don't* `folio_put()` again.  But, what if we *did* `folio_put()` and someone else *was* holding a reference?  We stole their reference. This is a severe breakage of reference counting.

Wait a second. What if `memory_failure` itself took a reference via `get_hwpoison_page()`?  If we just blindly drop *any* reference, we're in trouble.  Can a hugetlb page even be hardware poisoned *and* mapped? Yes! `memory_failure` sets `PG_hwpoison` early, then tries to unmap. If the unmap fails, the page remains mapped and `memory_failure` returns `MF_FAILED`. Or maybe it is soft-offlined which also sets `PG_hwpoison`. What if there's a race?  Process A triggers UCE, `memory_failure` runs and sets `PG_hwpoison`. At the *exact* same time, Process B offlines the page.  The offline process sees the page is poisoned and that its reference count is 2 (mapped + hwpoison) and it blindly drops *one* reference!  Then the offline process continues.  The page is *still* mapped by Process B (`refcount == 1`).  `memory_failure` finishes, maybe drops its reference. Refcount drops to 0!  But the page is still mapped by Process B!  Use-after-free!

This is a classic unconditional reference drop bug. We're dropping a reference we do not own.

Let me check the commit message: "To fix it, force to put ref of hwpoisoned hugetlb in memory offline". But it's doing more than that. It's not just skipping the page, it's actually dropping a reference, changing the lifecycle!  The commit mentions `b023f46813cd`, which "force[d] to skip hwpoisoned page regardless of refcount". Skipping a page is totally different from dropping a reference. The commit isn't just skipping, it's actively changing the lifecycle of the object by dropping a reference. I need to trace back `commit b023f46813cd` to understand this better.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerability in hugetlb Folio Handling**

Okay, so I've been digging into the `dequeue_hugetlb_folio_node_exact()` function and subsequent functions in the hugetlb folio allocation and deallocation path, and I've identified a significant potential vulnerability. It starts with how hardware-poisoned (hwpoisoned) folios are handled on the free list. A hwpoisoned folio on the free list is initially skipped by `alloc_hugetlb_folio()`. That's fine, but the implications downstream get tricky.

The free list maintenance and the `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` function are key here. A hwpoisoned folio will eventually hit `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` which then attempts to free the folio to the buddy allocator. *Crucially*, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` calls `folio_put()` which will decrement the reference count of the folio. `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` has a pre-condition that the folio isn't in use and that the reference count is zero.

The problem arises when a hwpoisoned folio is still mapped by a process but is also on the free list because of the interaction of `memory_failure()`. We know that `memory_failure()` takes a reference to the poisoned page if it's not unmapped. If it's *not* unmapped (e.g., due to `hwpoison_filter` or other reasons), the page gets its reference *leaked* from `memory_failure()`.

Now, if `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` gets called multiple times (as it would be if memory offlining is repeatedly attempted), each call to `folio_put(folio)` would decrement the refcount, even if the folio is still mapped. So even if the folio's reference count is greater than one because a user process is still using it, and we try to offline the memory, we call `folio_put()`. Then if `memory_offline` is called a *second* time, it will call `folio_put` *again*, decrementing the refcount, causing a refcount that should be 2 to be reduced to 1. If memory offlining is called again, we call folio_put a *third* time, decrementing a refcount from 1 to 0 and proceeding to `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` because the refcount is now zero. This is the race that leads to the vulnerability: a double-free/use-after-free scenario. This is because we're dissolving a hugetlb folio *while* the original process still has it mapped! This is a dangerous use-after-free condition.

The patch author's intention seems to be addressing an intentional reference leak from `memory_failure()`. Instead of carefully handling it, the author added `folio_put(folio)` multiple times in a loop. Even if there are a few references on the page, the loop leaks those references and drops the page to the buddy allocator while still in the page cache. This means that a memory offlining attempt could repeatedly try to dissolve the page, each time leaking a reference, potentially leading to the folio being dissolved while still in use. It appears they are sacrificing correctness for some presumed robustness but at the cost of memory corruption.

The implications are serious. This means an unprivileged user (if they can trigger memory offline, which is usually root, but still) or just a system administrator running a script, can corrupt memory and cause Use-After-Free. This is a significant security issue that needs immediate attention.

Now, if the folio is hardware-poisoned, does that function behave as expected?

Let's dive into `remove_mapping()`. Ah, there's that reference count check:

```c

			folio_nr_pages(folio) + folio_has_private(folio) + 1)
		return 0;
```

And I see that `memory_failure` has added a reference! So, `folio_ref_count` is elevated.  It looks like `remove_mapping()` will fail! It returns 0 and the folio *isn't* removed from the page cache. Aha! This is the root of the leak!

The process continues. The `memory_failure` reference means that when `shmem_truncate_range` or `hugetlbfs_evict_inode` calls `truncate_inode_pages`, the folio will *not* be removed. The elevated reference count trips up `remove_mapping`! So, the hardware-poisoned folio just stays in the page cache. Even during the final stages of inode deletion, `truncate_inode_pages_final` still can't remove the folio, because that extra reference still exists.

Okay, so `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache` calls `filemap_remove_folio(folio)`. That makes sense. But, `filemap_remove_folio()` *unconditionally* removes the folio from the page cache and *then* drops its reference. Interesting... and it's clear `truncate_inode_pages` isn't used by hugetlbfs directly. `hugetlbfs_evict_inode` uses `remove_inode_hugepages`.

`remove_inode_hugepages` gets folios via `filemap_get_folios` and then iterates, calling `remove_inode_single_folio()` on each. And here's where it gets interesting:

```c
			remove_inode_single_folio(h, inode, mapping, folio,
						  index, truncate_op);
			freed++;

			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
				folio_put(folio);
			}
```

Wait, hold on! If a folio is hardware poisoned, `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true, and it calls `folio_put(folio)`. But why is it doing that? Is this dropping the extra refcount added by `memory_failure` or the one added by `filemap_get_folios`?

Okay, let's break it down. `filemap_get_folios` retrieves a batch of folios. Each folio in that batch gets its reference count *incremented*. Later, `folio_batch_release(&fbatch)` will drop *that* reference, which was taken by `filemap_get_folios`.

So, the `folio_put(folio)` *inside* the loop is dropping an *extra* reference!

Okay, trace this: When the file is deleted (truncated), `remove_inode_hugepages` removes it from the pagecache. `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache` calls `filemap_remove_folio`, which drops the pagecache's reference. Then, if the folio is hwpoisoned, `folio_put(folio)` drops *another* reference. Finally, `folio_batch_release()` drops the `filemap_get_folios` reference.

So, if `memory_failure` had incremented the refcount by 1 for the hwpoison scenario, `remove_inode_hugepages` is already dropping it. But, wait... The patch commit message says "Even after the hugetlb file is deleted, the hugetlb folio is still leaked."? Why?

Does `me_huge_page()` increment the refcount by 2? No, `get_hwpoison_page()` gets 1 reference. Ah! `memory_failure` *does* increment the reference count:

```c
		extra_pins = true;
		folio_unlock(folio);

```

It doesn't call `__page_handle_poison(p)` if mapping is true! So where is `PageHWPoison` set? Ah! `memory_failure` sets `SetPageHWPoison(p)` BEFORE calling `me_huge_page`? Let's go back and examine `memory_failure()`...

    "thought": "**Hugetlb Folio Leak and Use-After-Free Vulnerability Analysis**

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic of `memory_failure()` and how it interacts with hugetlb folios, and I've found a critical issue. My initial thought process focused on whether the reference acquired by `get_hwpoison_page()` within `memory_failure()` is ever dropped. It turns out it's NOT explicitly dropped, which is intentional to prevent premature freeing of hwpoisoned pages, either because they are offlined or kept in the pagecache. However, I’ve discovered a subtle flaw.

Essentially, `memory_failure` keeps a reference on the hugetlb folio if it's mapped to a process. If it's *not* mapped, the reference is dropped (via `folio_put(folio)`), then handled via `__page_handle_poison(p)`. If it *is* mapped, the reference *is* kept, and the hwpoison folio in pagecache will have its refcount elevated by one. The problem arises when the associated file is deleted, or the memory gets offlined.

It seems `remove_mapping()` refuses to remove the hwpoisoned folio from the page cache because its reference count is > 1. But `hugetlbfs_evict_inode` uses `filemap_remove_folio()` which *unconditionally* removes the folio from the page cache. This is where it gets bad: if the file is deleted, and the folio *is* removed from the page cache, the `memory_failure` reference becomes the *only* reference. But if the `memory_failure` reference is dropped too early, we've got an orphaned folio – a memory leak! The author of the patch seems to have noticed this, but their fix is deeply flawed.

They hook into `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()`, which is called during memory offlining. This is a *horrible* place to fix a leak, because if the memory is never offlined, the leak persists forever!

Furthermore, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` iterates through *all* pfns in a memory block, and *unconditionally* calls `folio_put(folio)` on folios that are hugetlb and hwpoisoned if their refcount is > 0.

Here's the critical vulnerability:

*   If the file is NOT deleted, the folio is still in the page cache, and its refcount is at least 2 (page cache + `memory_failure`).
*   When memory offlining occurs, `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` *decrements* the refcount (using `folio_put()`)!
*   If `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` fails and gets called again, it *again* decrements the refcount, regardless of whether the file still exists or not.
*   Eventually, the refcount becomes 0, and `free_huge_folio()` is called, which frees the page, BUT IT'S STILL IN THE PAGE CACHE!
*   Now, if the file is accessed via the page cache or via process page tables, we have a *massive* Use-After-Free!

This patch *introduces* a Use-After-Free vulnerability by dropping a reference in a retryable function. The function's logic doesn't check whether the folio is truly leaked or still actively in use. Even if it's the leaked folio, calling it multiple times would drop references we don't own.

So, the guard is: `folio_test_hwpoison(folio) && folio_test_hugetlb(folio) && folio_ref_count(folio)`. We are analyzing if checking `folio_ref_count(folio)` is sufficient to safely call `folio_put(folio)`. This is not safe, because the refcount can be artificially inflated, and we can't reliably detect if we own it.

The analysis hinges on the fact that `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios()` can be called multiple times, the hugetlb folio can have artificially inflated reference count, and `folio_put()` unconditionally decrements the refcount. The Use-After-Free results from freeing the folio while it's still linked in the page cache xarray or mapped to the process.

I will be reporting this as a serious bug, with all the JSON data.
